source_paper_bibtex: @article{Bertschinger_2025, title={Flow Allocation Games}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0355}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0355}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Bertschinger, Nils and Hoefer, Martin and Schmand, Daniel}, year={2025}, month=feb, pages={68–89} }
context:
Section 5.1, Remark 21 (page 38 in the provided text pagination)

It is unclear whether the problem of deciding existence of a pure Nash equilibrium in an edge-ranking game is in NP or not, due to NP-hardness of verification that a node plays a best response (see Theorem 14). It is easy to see that the decision problem is in \(\Sigma_2^p\). The problem could be \(\Sigma_2^p\)-complete, similar to related decision problems in strategic max-flow games [33, 21]. Proving such a result is an interesting problem.
title: Classify the complexity of Nash equilibrium existence in edge-ranking flow games
background_latex:
A flow allocation game is specified by a directed graph \(G=(V,E)\), node supplies \((b_v)_{v\in V}\) with \(b_v\ge 0\), and edge capacities \((c_e)_{e\in E}\) with \(c_e\ge 0\). Each node \(v\in V\) is a strategic player.

In an edge-ranking strategy, each player \(v\) chooses a strict total order (a permutation) \(\pi_v\) of its outgoing edges \(E^+(v)=\{(v,w)\in E\}\). Given a profile \(\pi=(\pi_v)_{v\in V}\), a feasible flow is a vector \(f=(f_e)_{e\in E}\) with \(0\le f_e\le c_e\) such that for every node \(v\), if its total available supply is
\[
F_v \,=\, b_v + \sum_{e\in E^-(v)} f_e,
\]
then \(v\) sends flow along outgoing edges greedily according to \(\pi_v\): it fully saturates the highest-ranked outgoing edge until either that edge hits capacity or \(v\) runs out of supply, then proceeds to the next edge, etc. (Equivalently, \(f\) is a fixed point of the induced greedy allocation mapping.)

Among (possibly multiple) feasible flows for a given profile \(\pi\), the model selects the clearing state \(\hat f(\pi)\), defined as the coordinate-wise maximum feasible flow (the supremum in the lattice of feasible flows for monotone strategies). The utility of player \(v\) under \(\pi\) is the total outgoing flow in the clearing state:
\[
u_v(\pi) \,=\, \sum_{e\in E^+(v)} \hat f_e(\pi).
\]

A pure Nash equilibrium is a profile \(\pi\) such that for every player \(v\) and every alternative permutation \(\pi'_v\),
\[
u_v(\pi) \ge \nu_v(\pi'_v,\pi_{-v}).
\]

The decision problem of interest is: given \((G,(b_v),(c_e))\), does there exist a pure Nash equilibrium in edge-ranking strategies? Complexity is measured in the usual Turing model with \(b_v,c_e\) given in binary.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine the exact complexity class of the following decision problem.

Input: an edge-ranking flow allocation game \(\Gamma=(G,(b_v)_{v\in V},(c_e)_{e\in E})\).

Question: does there exist a pure Nash equilibrium profile \(\pi=(\pi_v)_{v\in V}\) of edge-ranking strategies (permutations of \(E^+(v)\)) with utilities \(u_v(\pi)=\sum_{e\in E^+(v)}\hat f_e(\pi)\) defined by the clearing state \(\hat f(\pi)\)?

In particular, decide whether this Nash-existence problem lies in \(\mathrm{NP}\), whether it is \(\Sigma_2^p\)-complete, or otherwise characterize its precise complexity.

Determine the exact complexity class of the following decision problem.

Input: an edge-ranking flow allocation game \(\Gamma=(G,(b_v)_{v\in V},(c_e)_{e\in E})\).

Question: does there exist a pure Nash equilibrium profile \(\pi=(\pi_v)_{v\in V}\) of edge-ranking strategies (permutations of \(E^+(v)\)) with utilities \(u_v(\pi)=\sum_{e\in E^+(v)}\hat f_e(\pi)\) defined by the clearing state \(\hat f(\pi)\)?

In particular, decide whether this Nash-existence problem lies in \(\mathrm{NP}\), whether it is \(\Sigma_2^p\)-complete, or otherwise characterize its precise complexity.
location_in_paper: Section 5.1, Remark 21 (page 38 in the provided text pagination)
open_problem_quote: It is unclear whether the problem of deciding existence of a pure Nash equilibrium in an edge-ranking game is in NP or not, due to NP-hardness of verification that a node plays a best response (see Theorem 14). It is easy to see that the decision problem is in \(\Sigma_2^p\). The problem could be \(\Sigma_2^p\)-complete, similar to related decision problems in strategic max-flow games [33, 21]. Proving such a result is an interesting problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is a complexity-classification problem rather than an existential conjecture about a finite structure. While one can search for reductions, automated instance search is not a direct way to resolve membership or completeness in \(\mathrm{NP}\) or \(\Sigma_2^p\).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could help explore candidate reductions (gadgets) from known \(\Sigma_2^p\)-complete problems and test them on small instances, but the fitness signal is only a heuristic proxy and the final step requires a formal complexity proof.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The forward-citation block provided contains no analyzed citing papers, so the only directly citable item here is the original paper itself. In that paper, edge-ranking flow allocation games are shown to have potentially no pure Nash equilibrium, and deciding existence is proved strongly NP-hard. Moreover, the authors note that NP-membership is unclear because verifying a best response can be NP-hard, but the existence problem is easily seen to lie in \(\Sigma_2^p\). They explicitly leave open whether the problem is in NP or \(\Sigma_2^p\)-complete.

With no forward-citation evidence available, there is currently no basis in the provided data to claim a resolution of the exact complexity classification. The most accurate status from the source is therefore that the problem remains open: the decision problem is strongly NP-hard and in \(\Sigma_2^p\), with the gap between NP and \(\Sigma_2^p\) (and potential \(\Sigma_2^p\)-completeness) unresolved.

Promising directions suggested by the source context include (i) proving \(\Sigma_2^p\)-hardness via a reduction from a canonical \(\exists\forall\)-QBF problem, likely by encoding the universal quantifier into the best-response verification subproblem, and (ii) alternatively proving NP-membership by establishing a polynomial-size certificate for equilibrium existence (e.g., a succinct representation of a profile together with a polynomial-time verifiable condition implying no profitable deviation), which would require overcoming the apparent hardness of best-response checking in the edge-ranking model.

sources.title: Flow Allocation Games
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces flow allocation games and analyzes equilibria for unit-ranking vs. edge-ranking strategies. For edge-ranking games, proves strong NP-hardness of deciding existence of pure Nash equilibrium and strong equilibrium, and shows membership of Nash-existence in \(\Sigma_2^p\) with the precise complexity left open (Remark 21).
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open problem: classify the exact complexity of pure Nash equilibrium existence in edge-ranking flow allocation games beyond strong NP-hardness and \(\Sigma_2^p\) membership.
